flags: The base center support does not close the seat frame: 16-3/4" plus the two 3/4" base side supports is 18-1/4", not the 19" the seat panel and the front apron both establish. The rewrite tells the builder to measure the actual gap rather than trusting the number.

The original upholstery step called for 2" screws to fasten the seat, but no 2" screw appears in materials[]; the rewrite fastens from below and warns about breaking through the 1/2" plywood instead of naming a length.

The base side supports carry an authored 8" off-square end meant to follow the back-leg rake, but the plan never states the back legs' rake angle, so the rewrite describes the orientation without asserting the angle they must match.
Board yield verified with packBoards: the 6 ft 1x4 (four aprons, 67" of 72"), the 8 ft 1x2, the 8 ft 2x2, the 8 ft 2x4 and the 3 ft 1x6 each fit in the single board listed.

step.title: Cut the rest of the parts
step.body: From the 1x4, cut two side aprons 17 1/2" long and two front and back aprons 16" long — that is 67" of the 72" board, so cut the long pieces first. From the 2x2, cut two front legs 17 1/4" and four corner braces 4" long with both ends at 45° and not parallel to each other. From the 1x2, cut the seat back top 16", two base side supports 19" (longest-point measurement, one end cut 8° off square so it can follow the rake of the back leg), and the base center support 16 3/4". From the 1x6, cut two seat back boards 16" long, and cut the plywood seat top panel to 19" x 19". Label the two 19" base supports as you cut them so the angled end does not get squared off by mistake later.

ops.body: Stand the two back legs on edge with their front cuts facing each other and set a 17 1/2" side apron against each leg, flush to the bottom of that front cut and flush to the leg's outside face. Fasten with pocket-hole screws driven from the inside of the apron into the leg, or predrill and screw through the leg — either way put the fasteners where the seat and upholstery will hide them. Before the glue sets, lay the two leg-and-apron assemblies on top of one another and check that they still match at the top, bottom, and front edges. That match is the one error you cannot correct later.

Set a 17 1/4" front leg at the front end of each side apron with the leg's top flush to the apron's top edge, so the two together give one flat surface for the seat frame. Glue and screw through the apron into the leg, predrilling first — 2x2 pine splits easily when a screw lands near a corner. Stand the two side assemblies up and sight across them: the front legs should be parallel and the tops of all four legs should land in one plane.

Join the two side assemblies with the 16" front and back aprons, tops flush with the tops of the side aprons. That 16" is what sets the chair's width: 16" between the two 1 1/2" front legs comes to 19" overall, matching the 19" x 19" plywood seat panel, so measure across the front before you drive anything. Use pocket holes drilled into the ends of each apron plus glue, and predrill if you screw through a face instead — 1x4 pine splits readily at the end grain. Check both diagonals across the seat frame and adjust until they match.

The 16" seat back top is the 1x2 that caps the two back legs and ties them together — the same 16" span the back apron uses. Set it across the legs, glue it, and drive two screws per end into predrilled holes so the 3/4" stock does not split. Measure from leg to leg at the seat and again up at the top: equal readings mean the back is not racked, and this is the last easy chance to correct it.

Fill the opening between the back legs with the two 16" 1x6 seat back boards, leaving a small even gap between them and below the seat back top so the back reads as slats rather than one solid panel. Fasten each board with pocket holes drilled from the back face, or predrill through the legs and screw in from outside. Because the back legs rake backwards, the boards sit on that rake — hold each one in place and mark it against the legs rather than measuring up from the floor.

The two 19" base side supports run the full length of the seat frame and the shorter base center support fits between them. Set each 19" support inside the frame with its 8° end against the raked back leg and its square end at the front, top edge level and low enough that the 1/2" plywood seat will finish flush with the tops of the aprons. Glue and screw them into the aprons and legs. These supports carry the sitter's weight, so they add real strength — they are not just a ledge for the seat to rest on.

Fit the base center support between the two side supports, midway front to back, and fasten with glue and a screw into each end. Measure the actual gap between your installed side supports before trimming it: 16 3/4" plus the two 3/4" supports comes to 18 1/4" rather than the frame's 19", so the listed length does not close on its own. Then glue and screw the four 4" corner braces into the inside corners of the apron frame, each brace's 45° ends bearing flat against the two aprons it spans. The braces are what stop the chair racking when someone leans back in it.

Spray-glue the 19" x 19" x 1" foam to the 19" x 19" plywood seat top with the edges aligned. Lay the fabric face-down on a table, centre the seat on it foam-side down, and pull the fabric up over the edges, stapling to the underside of the plywood with 3/8" staples — work the middle of each side first, then come back and fold each corner neatly instead of bunching it. Trim the excess so it will not foul the frame, then drop the seat onto the base supports and fasten from underneath through the supports, choosing a screw short enough that it cannot break through the 1/2" plywood and the foam above it.

Sand every exposed wood surface through the assorted grits and ease the sharp edges on the legs, aprons and back where hands and clothes will catch. Vacuum and wipe the dust off before finishing. Paint or stain as you like, and put the finish on the bottoms of the legs too — that is the surface that gets dragged across a floor.

baseStepTitles: Attach the front and back aprons

flags: CUT-LIST DEFECT: only two 72" face frame stiles are listed, but the description says to build two full face frames, and each frame needs a pair — four are required. The rewrite states this in the prose.

LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): the 1x2 parts (eighteen 21-3/4" narrow face boards, six 22-1/2" cross pieces, four 15-3/4" slide supports) need seven 8 ft boards; six are listed. And with the four stiles the plan actually requires, the 2x2 parts need nine 8 ft boards against the eight listed (seven suffice for the two stiles as literally written).

The optional back panel is listed 31" x 72" and noted as 'overall size minus 1/2"', but the face frame comes to 37-1/2" wide (34-1/2" rail plus two 1-1/2" stiles). The two do not agree, so the rewrite does not restate a back-panel size.
The 31" dividers and shelves likewise do not resolve against a 37-1/2"-wide frame; the rewrite tells the builder to measure each finished bay, which is also what the plan's own description instructs.
Verified as sufficient: the 1x3 face boards need exactly six 8 ft boards, matching the six listed.

step.title: Cut the frame and face stock
step.body: From the 2x2 cut the face frame stiles 72" long, eight rails 34 1/2", and nine dividers 22" — all nine dividers go in the front frame, since the back frame is built without them. From the 1x3 cut twenty-four wide face boards 21 3/4"; from the 1x2 cut eighteen narrow face boards 21 3/4", six cross pieces 22 1/2", and four drawer slide supports 15 3/4". Crosscut the ripped plywood strips into two sides 69", four dividers and shelves 31", and four corner posts 15 3/4" square. Two counts are worth checking before you buy: the cut list gives only two 72" stiles, but the front and back frames need a pair each, so cut four — and four stiles pushes the 2x2 requirement to nine 8 ft boards against the eight listed. The 1x2 is short as listed too; the narrow face boards, cross pieces and slide supports need seven 8 ft boards, not six.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Assemble each frame flat on the bench with two 72" stiles on the outside and the 34 1/2" rails between them, which makes each frame 37 1/2" wide overall. Set the pocket-hole jig for 1 1/2" stock, drill the ends of every rail and divider, and join with 2 1/2" pocket hole screws and glue. Space the four rails to give the three bay heights you want, then fit the nine 22" dividers into the front frame only — the back frame gets rails and stiles alone, because it never has to frame an individual drawer face. Measure both diagonals on each finished frame and rack it until they match; a frame that is out of square here cannot be corrected once the cabinet is standing.

Lay the front frame flat and build the door faces directly against it, using the 21 3/4" wide and narrow boards with the 22 1/2" cross pieces behind them — the frame is your reference surface, and squaring eleven thin boards against it now is far easier than trying to true them up after the cabinet is standing. Glue and pin each board to its cross pieces, keeping the gaps between boards consistent across the whole face. Dry-lay the drawer face boards in their openings at the same time and mark which group belongs to which drawer, because the openings will not all come out identical.

Stand the two frames parallel, 15 3/4" apart — the width of the ripped plywood — and join them at each corner with a 15 3/4" plywood corner post, glued and screwed into both frames. Work on a flat floor rather than a sagging bench, because a twist locked in now shows up later as doors that will not sit flush. Check the diagonals across the top and again across the bottom before the glue grabs; equal diagonals are the only reliable proof the box is square.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Set each 69" plywood side into the frames so it runs between the top and bottom rails — 69" plus the two 1 1/2" rails is the frame's full 72" height, which is how you know the side is the right part and the right way up. Keep its outside face flush with the outside of the stiles, then glue and screw through the side into the stiles and corner posts. Check the cabinet for square and each side for plumb as you fasten, not after.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Stand the 31" plywood dividers upright between the sides to split the cabinet into its three bays, keeping each one flush to the inside face of the front frame so nothing proud of it can foul a drawer or a door. Fasten with pocket holes from the back side, or with screws down through the rails, holding a square against the divider and the frame as you drive the first fastener. Then measure the finished opening in every bay and write the numbers down — every drawer and door from here on is built to those measurements, not to the plan's.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 15-3/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Fasten the four 15 3/4" 1x2 slide supports inside the two outer bays only — the centre bay's dividers already give the slides something to screw to. Run each support front to back, its full 15 3/4" matching the cabinet's depth so it lands flush at both ends, and set the pair in each bay level with one another. Check with a level across the two supports before the glue sets; a slide mounted on a support that slopes will bind under load no matter how well the drawer is built.

Build three drawer boxes from the 1x6, sizing each to the opening you measured minus whatever side clearance your slides require. Do not simply cut to the listed 19 1/2" sides and 17" fronts and backs — those assume perfectly flat boards and slides needing exactly 1/2" a side, which real lumber and real hardware rarely deliver. Join the corners with glue and 1 1/4" pocket hole screws, or glue and brads if you would rather not see pocket holes inside the box. Measure the diagonals on each box before the glue sets; a racked box binds in its slides however carefully the slides are mounted.

Mount the Euro bottom-corner slides to the 1x2 supports in the two outer bays and to the divider faces in the centre bay, keeping each pair level and at the same height front to back. Slide each box in so its front sits 3/4" back from the face frame — exactly the thickness of the face boards that go on next. Hang the doors on the inset hinges last, shimming each until the gap around it looks even on all four sides, and check every drawer runs to full extension before you commit to the hinge screw positions.
materials: Euro style bottom corner mount drawer slides, 16 in, pair

ops.body: Fasten each assembled face panel to its drawer box from inside the box, so no fastener shows on the front. Set the face with an even 1/8" gap on all four sides, using scraps or coins of that thickness as shims under and beside it while you drive the first two screws, rather than eyeballing it. Open and close each drawer before moving to the next; across a run of faces this size, one set a hair out of line is obvious from across the room.

baseStepTitles: Build the doors and test drawer faces

flags: CUT-LIST DEFECT: the row named 'Back panel' is a 3/4" x 3-1/2" x 54" PINE board, not a panel. The plan therefore gives no dimension for the 1/4" plywood back that materials[] and the assembly step both call for. The rewrite tells the builder to measure the assembled case instead.

The face frame parts do not close to one rectangle: the 51" top rail between two 1-1/2" stiles is 54", while the bottom rail is 57-1/2" and the case top is 57-1/2". The rewrite says to lay the frame out against the finished case front and cut the rails to what is actually measured.

run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves no box for this plan — the 52-1/2" shelf closes against neither a 57-1/2" full-width top nor a top set between the sides. The rewrite deliberately does not assert which member is captured.
The back support is 2-1/2" wide (a 1x3), but the original step tagged the 1x4; corrected in the rewrite.
Crown moulding is tight: the front (57-1/2") plus two sides (11-3/4" each) is 81" against the 83" supplied, with no allowance for miter waste. Stated in the prose.
Board yield verified with packBoards: the four 1x2 face frame parts need three 8 ft boards, matching the three listed.

step.title: Cut the case and frame parts
step.body: Break the 3/4" plywood into 11 3/4"-wide strips, then crosscut two sides 46 3/4", the top 57 1/2", the shelf 52 1/2", and the divider 10 3/4". Cut with the good face up and support the offcut so the sheet cannot twist as it comes free. From the pine cut two face frame stiles 43 1/2", the top rail 51", and the bottom rail 57 1/2" from the 1x2, and the 52 1/2" back support from the 1x3. Leave the crown moulding uncut until the case is standing: its three pieces total 81" against the 83" of stock supplied, so there is almost nothing to spare for a mis-cut miter.

ops.body: Stand the two 46 3/4" sides on edge and join the 57 1/2" top to them with glue and pocket-hole screws or countersunk screws driven down through the top. The plan's own numbers do not settle whether the top caps the sides or sits between them — the 52 1/2" shelf closes on neither reading — so dry-fit it, decide now, and keep the shelf and divider consistent with whatever you chose. Check both diagonals across the assembly and rack it until they match before the glue sets.

Set the 52 1/2" shelf across the case at the height your components need, and stand the 10 3/4" divider on it to split the lower bay. If the shelf does not land tight against both sides, trim it to the inside measurement you actually have — your case is the governing number, not the cut list. Fasten both with pocket holes drilled on their undersides and hidden faces, plus glue, and hold a square where the divider meets the shelf; the divider is short enough that a small lean is easy to miss and will show against the face frame later.

Run the 52 1/2" back support across the back of the case, flush with the back edges of the sides, and fasten it with glue and pocket-hole screws into each side. It does two jobs: it stiffens the case against racking, and it gives the thin plywood back something solid to nail into. Put it where a brad nailer can reach it once the back goes on, and check the case for square again after it is fastened — a support fitted into a racked case will hold the rack permanently.

Build the face frame flat on the bench first — the two 43 1/2" stiles, the 51" top rail, and the 57 1/2" bottom rail — joined with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws. These four lengths do not add up to one rectangle: 51" between two 1 1/2" stiles is 54", while the bottom rail is 57 1/2". So lay the parts out against the finished case front, mark the rails there, and cut them to what you actually measure. Once the frame is square, glue it to the front of the case and pin it with 1 1/4" brads, keeping the outside edges flush with the case sides.

Cut the 1/4" plywood back to the outside dimensions of the assembled case — measure the case, because the cut list's only 'Back panel' row is a 3/4" x 3 1/2" pine board and gives you no panel size. Nailing the back on is what locks the case square, so check the diagonals one last time and rack the case gently by hand until they match before driving more than the first few nails. Run glue along the sides, top, shelf and back support, then pin the panel with 1 1/4" brads every few inches.

Sand every surface through the assorted grits, leaving the face frame and the crown until last so you do not round over the mitered corners. Ease the front edges of the top and shelf slightly — a crisp plywood arris chips the first time something is dragged across it. Vacuum and wipe the dust off, then prime and paint or stain and seal. Finish the inside of the bays too; they get handled every time a component is swapped.

baseStepTitles: Attach the top to the sides
Build and attach the face frame
Cut and attach the crown moulding

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL: the twelve 2x4 parts need ten 8 ft boards, but nine are listed. Proof: each of the four 77" joists takes its own board (two cannot share 96"); the two 10-1/2" spacers ride along with two of them. Of the remaining six pieces (60", 60", 57", 57", 57", 40") no pair fits a 96" board with a 1/8" kerf — 57" + 40" is 97-1/8" — so they need six more boards. Four plus six is ten.

The 40" headboard top joist plus the two 10-1/2" top spacers comes to 61", which does not close the headboard's 64" width; the plan does not determine where those three pieces sit, so the rewrite carries the original wording without asserting a placement.

The upholstery step calls for pieces of batting, burlap AND fabric each roughly 5 yards long, while materials[] lists 6 yards of the three combined; the yardage may not cover the headboard plus the siderails and footrail. Not resolvable from the plan's data.

Verified sound and stated in the prose: 77" joists plus two 1-1/2" rails equals the 80" siderails; the 60" rails equal the slat length; 61-1/2" footrail equals 60" plus two 3/4" siderail thicknesses; three 57" joists plus two 3-1/2" legs equals the 64" headboard panel.

step.title: Cut the framing lumber
step.body: From the 2x4 cut three headboard joists 57", one headboard top joist 40", two top spacers 10 1/2", two bed frame top and bottom rails 60", and four bed frame joists 77". From the 4x4 cut two headboard legs 49" and two bottom legs 13" — each 49" leg needs its own 8 ft post, and the 13" legs come out of the offcuts. Cut the panel cleat 28" from the 1x4, the two siderails 80" and the footrail 61 1/2" from the 1x12, and ten mattress slats 60" from the 1x3. Count your 2x4s before you start: the four 77" joists take a full 8 ft board each, and none of the 60" or 57" pieces will pair up on a 96" board once you allow for the saw kerf, so the twelve 2x4 parts need ten boards, one more than the nine listed.

ops.body: Lay the two 49" 4x4 legs on the floor and run the three 57" joists between them — one at the top, one at the bottom, one in the middle. That gives 57" plus two 3 1/2" posts, or 64" overall, exactly the 64" edge of the plywood headboard panels, so measure it before you fasten. Predrill each 4x4 in two stages: first with a bit sized to the screw head, halfway through, then the rest of the way with a bit sized to the shank, so the head pulls down into the post instead of splitting it. Glue every joint and drive 3 1/2" screws. Add the 40" top joist and the two 10 1/2" top spacers across the top of the frame as shown; if you substitute doubled 2x4s for the 4x4 posts, add 1" to each joist length so the finished width stays the same.

Lay the four 77" joists between the two 60" top and bottom rails: 77" plus the two 1 1/2" rails is 80", exactly the length of the siderails, and the 60" rails set the width, which is also the length of every mattress slat. Put the two outer joists flush with the ends of the rails and space the other two evenly between them. Predrill and drive 3 1/2" screws through the face of each rail into the end grain of each joist, with glue at every joint. Measure both diagonals across the finished rectangle and adjust until they match — a frame out of square will not take the slats evenly and telegraphs straight through the mattress.

Set a 13" 4x4 leg into each bottom corner of the foot end, tight into the corner where the rail meets the joist so the leg bears against two members rather than one. Predrill in the same two stages you used on the headboard posts, then toenail 3 1/2" screws in at an angle from the leg into both the rail and the joist. The headboard's own 49" legs carry the head end, so these two are all the frame needs — check the bed sits flat on the floor without rocking before you go further.

Wrap each 80" siderail in burlap, then batting, then fabric, stapling to the inside face where nothing shows, and fold the corners rather than gathering them — that fold is the whole difference between a tailored rail and a lumpy one. Trim the excess back so it cannot get pinched under the mattress. Then screw each rail to the outside of the frame with 2 1/2" screws, keeping the top edges level with one another; at 11 1/4" wide the rails stand well above the frame and any difference between them shows the length of the bed.

Wrap the 61 1/2" footrail exactly as you did the siderails. Its length is deliberate — 60" of frame plus the 3/4" thickness of each siderail is 61 1/2" — so it laps across the ends of both rails and closes the foot of the bed flush. Screw through the footrail into the frame's end rail with 2 1/2" screws, driving them where the mattress will hide the heads.

Lay the ten 60" 1x3 slats across the frame — they span the full 60" width — and space them evenly along its 80" length so no gap between two slats is wider than a slat, which is what stops a mattress sagging between them. Screw each end down into the frame, predrilling near the ends so the thin 1x3 does not split. In a humid room, slats or an actual box spring let air move under the mattress; solid plywood traps moisture and invites mildew, which is why this bed does not use one.
Attach the panel-frame plywood piece (cut with the headboard panels) to the front of the headboard frame with 2" screws and glue.

baseStepTitles: Upholster the outside of the headboard frame
Upholster and hang the center panel
Join the headboard to the frame

flags: CUT-LIST DEFECT: the original 'Build the espresso machine box' step calls for a bottom, but no espresso machine bottom exists in the cut list. The rewrite builds the box from the parts that are actually listed.

The espresso machine box does not close on the plan's numbers: the 9-1/2" sides plus two 3/4" thicknesses is 11", not the 12" of the front and back. run1-box-geometry.mjs resolves only the trim relation (the 12" front/back run full length, the 2" small trim sits between them), which the rewrite follows and does not contradict.

TWO EXACT-LENGTH BOARDS, no waste allowance: the 24" cart centre panel consumes the whole 2 ft 1x10, and the 12" wide detail board the whole 1 ft 1x4. Neither leaves room to square a factory end. Stated in the prose.

The 26-1/2" cart top support is 1" longer than the cart's 25-1/2" outside width; the plan does not say how that inch is distributed, so the rewrite offers 1/2" a side and tells the builder to mark against the actual assembly.
Board yield verified with packBoards: the 10 ft 1x12 holds all five cart panels (117" of 120"), the 4 ft 1x8 both machine sides and front/back, and the 4 ft 1x2 all four of its parts.

step.title: Cut all the parts
step.body: From the 10 ft 1x12 cut the cart bottom 25 1/2", two sides 17 1/2", the shelf 24", and the top 32 1/2" — that is 117" of the 120" board, so cut longest first and do not waste an end. Cut the 24" centre panel from the 1x10 and the espresso machine sides 9 1/2" and front and back 12" from the 1x8. From the 1x2 cut the cart top support 26 1/2", the machine top 13 1/2", and two small trim pieces 2"; from the 1x3 cut the narrow detail board 12" and two small blocks 3 1/2". Cut four 3" legs, one 1 1/2" cup holder and two 1/2" knobs from the 2" dowel, and a 5" handle and 6" steam wand from the 3/4" dowel. Two parts use their whole board with nothing to spare — the 24" centre panel is the entire 2 ft 1x10 and the 12" wide detail board the entire 1 ft 1x4 — so if either factory end is not square you will need a little extra stock.

ops.body: Stand the 25 1/2" bottom board good-face-down and set a 3" dowel leg in each corner, held in far enough from the edges that the leg sits fully under the board. Predrill into the end of each dowel first — a screw driven into unpredrilled end grain will split it — then drive two 2" self-tapping screws down through the bottom into each leg. Two per leg, not one, or the leg will pivot the first time the cart is pushed. Stand the base on a flat floor and check it does not rock before anything else goes on.

Set a 17 1/2" side upright on the bottom board at each end, outside faces flush with the ends of the 25 1/2" bottom. That leaves exactly 24" between the two sides, which is why the shelf and the centre panel are both 24" long — measure it before you fasten. Glue and drive 1 1/4" brads down through each side into the bottom, then stand the 24" centre panel between the sides midway along and nail it the same way. The centre panel is what reinforces the whole cart, so the earlier joints do not have to carry the load on their own.

Rest the 24" shelf on the centre panel and between the sides, check it level, and glue and nail through the sides into its ends. Run the 26 1/2" top support across the sides — it is 1" longer than the cart's 25 1/2" outside width, so let it project 1/2" at each end, or hold it in place and mark it to whatever your assembly actually measures. Then centre the 32 1/2" top on the cart: at 7" longer than the 25 1/2" bottom it overhangs 3 1/2" at each end, which is what gives the cart its counter. Glue and pin the top down with 1 1/4" brads into the sides, centre panel and top support.

Lay the machine's profile out on one 9 1/2" x 7 1/4" piece of 1x8 and cut it with the jigsaw, then trace that finished piece onto the second and cut the second slightly proud of the line. Clamp the two together and sand them as a pair down to the line so they end up genuinely identical — the box only closes cleanly if both sides match. Keep them stacked while you sand the sawn edges smooth; this is a toy that small hands will grip, so nothing should be left rough or sharp.

Assemble the box from the two shaped 9 1/2" sides and the two 12" front and back pieces, with the 13 1/2" 1x2 across the top. Note before you glue that the numbers do not quite close — 9 1/2" plus two 3/4" thicknesses is 11", not the 12" of the front and back — so dry-fit the four pieces and decide how you want the overhang to fall before committing. Glue every joint and pin with 1 1/4" brads driven from the faces you will not see. Check the front for square: the detail boards that go on next are all cut to 12", the same as the front, and will only sit flush if it is true.

Glue and pin the 12" wide detail board and the 12" narrow detail board across the front of the machine, edges flush with the front's own edges since both are cut to its full 12". Add the two 3 1/2" small blocks where the group head and drip tray go, and fit the 2" trim pieces between the full-length front and back rather than over them. Use 5/8" brads on the thinner pieces so nothing can point out of the back face, and glue everything — on a toy, glue is what holds and the brads only stop it sliding while the glue cures.

Cut the dowels on the miter saw with the stock clamped down or held in a V-block — a round dowel will roll into the blade if you hold it by hand. Glue the 1 1/2" length of 2" dowel to the front as the cup holder and the two 1/2" discs of the same dowel on as control knobs. For the 5" handle and the 6" steam wand, drill a shallow socket for each so the dowel seats into the wood instead of relying on a glue joint to end grain, which is the weakest joint there is. A child will pull on all four of these, so glue every one and let them cure fully before the toy goes into use.

Sand the whole cart and the machine, rounding every corner and easing every edge — this is handled by small hands, so nothing should be left sharp. Fill the brad holes, sand them flush, and vacuum the dust off. Prime and paint with a finish rated safe for children's furniture, and let it cure fully rather than just dry to the touch before the cart is played with.

baseStepTitles: Attach the sides and center panel
Add the shelf, support, and top
Add the detail boards and blocks

flags: The plan describes the towel-bar opening as four drilled corners joined with a jigsaw — a rectangular cut-out — while materials[] call for a 1" round closet rod. The two do not describe the same hole. The rewrite sizes the rod from the plan's own numbers but does not resolve the opening's shape.
The plan gives no wall-mounting method and no mounting hardware appears in materials[]; the rewrite says to hit studs or use rated anchors without naming a fastener.

Verified sound and stated in the prose: the 22-1/2" shelf plus two 3/4" sides is 24", exactly the back panel's width; the 25" drop ledge is 1" longer than that 24", which is the plan's own 1/2" overhang at each end; a 24" closet rod therefore finishes flush with both outside faces.
Board yield verified with packBoards: all five 1x6 parts (about 80") fit the single 8 ft board, and the four 1x2 parts fit the single 8 ft 1x2.

step.title: Cut the remaining parts
step.body: With the sides shaped, cut the rest of the 1x6: the shelf 22 1/2" and the two roof cap pieces 12 1/2", each with its top end beveled 30° off square. Those 12 1/2" and 16 1/4" figures are longest-point measurements, so set the saw to 30° and read the long face of the board, not the short one. All five 1x6 parts together use about 80" of the 8 ft board, so there is room to recut a bevel if the first one goes wrong. From the 1x2 cut two roof trim pieces 16 1/4" with a 30° top end and two drop ledge pieces 25". Cut the back panel 24" x 13 3/4" from the 1/4" plywood or beadboard.

ops.body: Stand both sides on edge with their 30° bevels pointing up and away from each other, and set the 22 1/2" shelf between them. That length is deliberate: 22 1/2" plus the two 3/4" sides comes to 24", exactly the width of the back panel, so if the assembly does not measure 24" across the outside the back will not fit. Glue and drive two or three 2" finish nails through the outside of each side into the shelf's end grain, predrilling near the ends so the pine does not split. Check the shelf is square to both sides before the glue sets.

Glue and nail the two 16 1/4" roof trim pieces along the outer edges of the roof cap, their 30° ends meeting at the peak the same way the cap boards do. Dry-fit the pair at the peak first: if the two angled ends leave a gap, shave one with a light pass on the saw rather than forcing the joint closed with nails, because a forced miter opens again as the glue dries. Use 1 1/4" finish nails here — the trim is only 3/4" thick over another 3/4" board, and a 2" nail will come through.

Glue and nail the two 25" drop ledge pieces beneath the main shelf to form the lower surface for folded towels. At 25" they are 1" longer than the 24" outside width of the case, which is exactly the 1/2" overhang at each end — measure that overhang at both ends before nailing rather than centring by eye. Drive 1 1/4" finish nails up into the shelf and back into the sides so no fastener shows from the front.

Cut the 1" closet rod to 24" so it passes through both sides and finishes flush with their outside faces — 22 1/2" between the sides plus the two 3/4" thicknesses. Slide it through, check it sits level and has not bowed the sides in or out, then glue it in place. Wipe any squeeze-out off the rod before it dries: a bead of hardened glue is exactly where a towel will catch and snag.

If you want hanging hooks, mount the double robe hooks on the front face below the shelf, spaced evenly and screwed into the solid 3/4" pine rather than into the 1/4" back. Double hooks fit tighter wall spaces than the larger single hooks shown as an alternative, which is why they are the better choice in a small bathroom. When you hang the finished shelf, drive the mounting screws through the back panel into wall studs, or use anchors rated for the load — a loaded towel bar pulls far harder on a wall than the shelf's own weight suggests.

baseStepTitles: Cut the two sides as mirror images

flags: LUMBER SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): the 1x2 parts — four side trim at 11-1/2", three shelf trim and top support at 14-3/4", one top trim at 17-3/4" — total 108" and need two 8 ft boards. Materials list one.

The tall dividers are 33-1/4" inside a carcass whose sides are 34" with a 3/4" top over them; the plan does not state the clearance the grid needs to lift out past the bottom shelf, so the rewrite tells the builder to measure the finished opening before cutting any grid piece.

Neither the slot width nor the slot spacing on the interlocking dividers is authored anywhere in the plan; the rewrite gives the technique (stack-cut, test on an offcut, cut a hair over 1/4") without inventing a dimension.
Verified sound and stated in the prose: the 17-3/4" top less two 1-1/2" legs is exactly the 14-3/4" shelf length, which is also the short dividers' length.
Verified as sufficient: the 3/4" plywood half sheet, the two 8 ft 2x2s (four 34" legs), and the 2 ft 1x3 all cover their parts.

step.title: Cut the carcass parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 11 1/2"-wide strips, then crosscut two sides 34", the shelf 14 3/4", and the top 17 3/4". From the 2x2 cut four legs 34" — the same length as the sides, so their tops and bottoms will finish flush. From the 1x2 cut four side trim pieces 11 1/2", three shelf trim and top support pieces 14 3/4", and one top trim 17 3/4"; cut the 17 3/4" top front trim from the 1x3. Buy two 8 ft 1x2s rather than the one listed: those eight pieces come to 108" and a single 8 ft board is only 96". Cut the 1/4" plywood back 17 3/4" x 34 3/4" now, but leave the wine-grid dividers uncut until the carcass is assembled and measured.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

ops.body: Stand a 34" 2x2 leg at each front and back corner of both sides, outside faces flush with the side panel's front and back edges, and fasten through the pocket holes you drilled with 1 1/4" screws and glue. Because the legs and the sides are both 34", their tops and bottoms should finish flush — clamp each leg to its panel and check that before driving a screw. A leg set even a sixteenth proud will hold the top off the carcass and show as a gap along the whole front.

Drill pocket holes in both ends of the 14 3/4" shelf and fasten it between the sides, flush to the bottom, with 1 1/4" pocket-hole screws and glue, then nail its 1x2 trim across the front. That 14 3/4" is what sets the opening: the 17 3/4" top less a 1 1/2" leg at each front corner is exactly 14 3/4", which is also the length of every short wine-grid divider. Measure the finished opening once the shelf is in and write it down — the grid has to slide into that number, not into the drawing's.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

ops.body: Set the 17 3/4" top across the carcass flush to the front and fasten it down through the pocket holes in the top edges of the sides. Nail the 17 3/4" 1x2 top trim and the 17 3/4" 1x3 top front trim on with 1 1/4" finish nails and glue, keeping the outside edges flush so the front reads as a single thickness rather than a stack. Wipe the squeeze-out off now — glue left in a corner will refuse stain later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, half sheet, ripped into 11-1/2" strips

ops.body: Stack the three tall divider blanks — 13 3/4" wide by 33 1/4" long — clamp them together with bar clamps, and cut every interlocking slot through the stack at once so all three land in identical places. Cut the slots a hair wider than 1/4": nominal 1/4" plywood is rarely a true quarter inch, and a slot cut exactly to the nominal size will jam rather than slide. Test one slot on an offcut before committing to the stack. At 33 1/4" the tall dividers stand clear of the 34" sides, which is what lets the assembled grid lift back out.

Cut the seven short dividers 14 3/4" long by 13 3/4" wide, clamped as a stack the same way, with their slots spaced to match the tall dividers'. Cut these slots in from the opposite edge — one set slots down and the other up, and that opposition is the whole mechanism of the interlock. Dry-fit one tall and one short piece together before you cut the slots in the rest of the stack.

Slide the tall and short dividers together on a flat surface, working from one end so each joint seats fully before you add the next. Leave the grid completely dry — no glue and no fasteners — because coming apart is the entire point of the interlocking joint. Check that the assembled grid measures no more than the opening you recorded earlier, and ease the slots with sandpaper if anything is tight rather than forcing it home.

Slide the assembled grid into the hutch through the front. Keep the small shelf pieces removable from the front so you can later pull the shelves out, collapse the tall dividers, and lift the whole grid back out for cleaning or rearranging. Load a bottle into each of several slots before anything is fastened permanently — a wine bottle is longer and heavier than it looks on paper, and a slot that will not take one is far easier to open up now than after the back is on.

baseStepTitles: Drill pocket holes and trim the sides
Build and attach the bottom shelf
Attach the back and secure the hutch

flags: CUT-LIST CONTRADICTION: the roof panels row gives quantity 2 at 36" x 48", but its own note says one is 48" x 36" and the other 48" x 32". The rewrite refuses to pick a side and tells the builder to measure the assembled roofline.

The description says every truss is a different angle combination, but the cut list supplies ONE bottom-chord length and TWO brace lengths shared across all four trusses. What actually differs between trusses is not determined by the plan's data; the rewrite says so rather than inventing the difference.
The plan gives no door-opening dimensions even though door trim is listed at 25-3/4" top and 18-1/2" sides; the rewrite cuts the opening while the panel is accessible without asserting a size.
materials[] lists 'Pocket hole screws, 2-1/2"' but no pocket-hole jig appears in tools[]; the rewrite calls them countersunk screws, matching the original step's own wording.
No roofing, felt, or exterior finish appears in materials[] for a project that lives outdoors.

Board yield verified with packBoards: the twenty-six 2x2 members pack into nine 8 ft boards against eleven listed (two spare); the eleven 1x3 trim pieces need exactly the four listed; the four 1x2 pieces fit the one listed. Plywood: three 4x8 sheets cover the floor, both side panels, both gable panels and both roof panels.

step.title: Cut and label every part
step.body: Cut and label all the truss members before assembling anything — four trusses in several angle combinations are impossible to sort out later from a pile of look-alike sticks. From the 2x2 cut six top members 34 1/2" and six 30 1/2", each with its top end at 45° off square, long-point measurement; four bottom chords 29" with both ends at 10° off square and NOT parallel; four braces 23 3/4" with 30°/10° compound ends; four braces 21 3/8" with 40°/10° compound ends; and two ridge supports 48". Those twenty-six pieces pack into nine 8 ft boards, so the eleven listed leave two spare — cut a practice angle on a spare before touching the real stock. From the 1x3 cut two side trim 37", two straight front and back trim 29 3/4" (10° both ends, not parallel), two angled trim 23", two angled trim 25 1/4", one door trim top 25 3/4", and two door trim sides 18 1/2" with one end at 5°. From the 1x2 cut two lower side trim 18 3/4" and two upper 21 1/2". Break the plywood down last: the floor 29" x 39", the lower side panel 39" x 19 1/4", the upper 39" x 22", and the two gable panels 42 3/8" x 40 3/8". Leave the roof panels until the roofline is standing — the cut list gives both as 36" x 48" while its own note says one is 48" x 32", so that pair has to be measured rather than trusted.

ops.body: Build each truss flat on the floor: the 34 1/2" and 30 1/2" top members meeting at the peak on their 45° ends, the 29" bottom chord across the base with its two non-parallel 10° ends, and one 23 3/4" and one 21 3/8" brace between them. The long and short top members are what throw the ridge off centre, so keep track of which side each goes on and build all four trusses the same way round. Predrill every end before driving a screw — 2x2 splits at the end grain, and a split member on a compound-angle joint cannot be pulled tight afterwards. Glue every joint with the waterproof glue and fasten with countersunk 2 1/2" screws. Lay the finished trusses on one another and record how yours actually differ; the description calls each one unique, but the cut list gives all four the same chord and brace lengths, so what varies is worth knowing before the panels go on.

Mark the four truss locations on the 29" x 39" floor panel, measuring every mark from the same end so the spacing cannot creep. The floor's 29" dimension matches the 29" bottom chords, so each truss sits square across the panel's width — that agreement is your check that you have the panel the right way round. Run a bead of waterproof glue where each truss lands, stand the trusses on their marks, and drive two or three 2" screws down through the plywood into each chord. Brace the assembly upright while the glue cures; a house this shape has almost no resistance to racking until the panels are on.

Trace the curved gable shapes from the plan's pattern onto the two 42 3/8" x 40 3/8" panels rather than eyeballing them — these are the faces of the house and any wobble in the curve is visible from the street — then cut them with the jigsaw, supporting the offcut so it cannot tear as it drops away. Cut the door opening in the front panel now, while the panel is still flat and reachable from both sides. Set each panel against its end truss, glue every bearing edge with the waterproof glue, and fasten with 2" screws into the truss members.

Attach the 39" x 19 1/4" lower side panel and the 39" x 22" upper panel with 2" screws and waterproof glue, lower panel first so the upper laps over it and sheds water outward rather than into the seam. Both are 39" long, matching the floor's 39" dimension, so their ends should land flush with the front and back panels. Screw into every truss the panel crosses, not just the ends.

Trim the front and back the same way, fitting the 29 3/4" straight pieces first and then the 23" and 25 1/4" angled pieces up the gable. Every one of these is a long-point measurement on a compound-angled end, so hold each piece against the actual panel and mark the cut there rather than trusting the tape — this is exactly where a sloppy cut turns charmingly crooked into actually does not fit. Glue with the waterproof formula and pin with 1" finish nails.

Attach the front and back overhang trusses so they land the same way the main trusses do — if they sit even slightly out of plane, the roof plywood will not lie flat across them. Join the tops with screws and waterproof glue, and dry-fit the whole roofline before committing any fastener. Add the two 48" ridge supports between the trusses; they are not strictly required, but on a roof this shape they are cheap insurance and they give the roof panels something to land on between rafters.

Fit the two roof panels last, dry-laid and held with spring clamps before any fastener goes in. Because the truss angles differ side to side the two panels are not the same size, and the plan disagrees with itself about how: the cut list gives both as 36" x 48" while its own note says one is 48" x 32". Measure your assembled roofline and cut both panels to what you actually have. Run waterproof glue along every bearing edge and drive 2" screws down into the top members and ridge supports, spaced evenly along each rafter. Leave the panels slightly proud at the eaves rather than short — an overhang throws water away from the walls, a flush edge runs it straight down them.

flags: PLYWOOD SHORTFALL (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): the seven 3/4" plywood parts are all 20-1/4" wide and total 224-1/2" of strip, needing three 96" strips. A 4x8 sheet yields only two 20-1/4" strips, i.e. 192" — about 32-1/2" short. One sheet is listed.

DRAWER DEFECT: the listed drawer sides are 5-1/2" and the bottom is 4-3/4" x 17-3/4", making a box roughly 5-3/4" deep front to back, while the specified slides are 16". The box as listed cannot use the specified hardware. The rewrite tells the builder to build to the measured opening and names the conflict.

DOOR PANEL DEFECT: the door frame (two 2-1/2" stiles plus a 9-3/4" rail) leaves a 9-3/4" x 11" opening, but the listed panel is 10-3/4" x 16" — it cannot sit inside the frame. The rewrite says to dry-fit and decide whether the panel is applied behind the frame.
The summary describes one door and one drawer, while the cut list (four stiles, four rails, two panels) and the hinge count (two pairs) describe two doors. Named in the prose; not resolved.
The original slide step said to attach the slides 'with nails and glue'; drawer slides are screwed so they stay adjustable and removable. Corrected in the rewrite.

Verified sound and stated in the prose: 45" rails plus two 1-1/2" stiles is the 48" width; 46-1/2" bottom plus two 3/4" sides is the same 48"; and 12-1/2" + 3/4" + 20" + 3/4" + 12-1/2" is exactly 46-1/2", which fixes the divider positions. run1-box-geometry.mjs confirms the 45" rails sit BETWEEN the full-length members.
Verified as sufficient: the 1x2 parts need exactly the three 8 ft boards listed (zero slack on one board), the 1x4 parts one board, and the 1x3 door parts one 12 ft board.

step.title: Cut the carcass, frame and door parts
step.body: Rip the 3/4" plywood into 20 1/4"-wide strips and crosscut two sides 35", the bottom 46 1/2", two inner dividers 31 1/2", two end shelves 12 1/2", and the centre open shelf 20". Check your sheet count before you start: those seven parts need about 224 1/2" of 20 1/4"-wide strip, and a 4x8 sheet yields only two full-length strips, or 192" — roughly 32 1/2" short, so plan on a second sheet or another source for the last parts. From the 1x2 cut two front-to-back nailers 20 1/4", two face frame stiles 35", two top and bottom rails 45", two centre shelf face stiles 5 3/4", and two door and drawer opening stiles 22 3/4". From the 1x4 cut two short back supports 12 1/2", the centre back support 20", and the kick rail 45". Cut four door stiles 16" and four door rails 9 3/4" from the 1x3, and two door panels 10 3/4" x 16" from the 1/2" plywood. Leave the drawer box stock uncut — it gets sized to the opening you end up with.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Stand the two 35" sides on the 46 1/2" bottom — 46 1/2" plus the two 3/4" sides is the vanity's 48" overall width, so confirm that number before driving a screw. Set the two 31 1/2" inner dividers between them so the interior reads 12 1/2", divider, 20", divider, 12 1/2": that adds to exactly 46 1/2", which is how you know the dividers are where they belong. Fit the two 12 1/2" end shelves into the outer bays at the heights you want. Drill 3/4" pocket holes and fasten everything with 1 1/4" pocket hole screws and glue, then measure both diagonals across the assembled box and rack it square before the glue sets.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Hold the 20" centre shelf up to your actual plumbing and mark the supply lines and the drain on its back edge, then cut those notches before the shelf ever goes in — the cutout is sized to your rough-in, not to a number on a plan. Cut generously: a notch slightly too big disappears behind the trap, one slightly too small means pulling the shelf back out after the face frame is on. Install it fixed with pocket holes, or drill shelf-pin holes in both dividers if you would rather it move later.
materials: 3/4" plywood, ripped to 20-1/4" strips, full sheet

ops.body: Fasten the two 12 1/2" and one 20" 1x4 back supports across the back of the carcass, one per bay, flush with the back edges of the sides and dividers — their lengths match the bay widths exactly, so a support that will not fit means a divider has moved. Use pocket holes drilled on their back faces so nothing shows inside the cabinet. Then run the two 20 1/4" front-to-back nailers across the top of the carcass; 20 1/4" is the full depth of the plywood parts, so they land flush front and back. Those nailers are what the countertop screws into, so put them where a driver can actually reach them once the top is on.

Build the face frame flat on the bench: two 35" stiles outside, the two 45" top and bottom rails between them — 45" plus the two 1 1/2" stiles is the 48" the carcass already measures — plus the 45" kick rail, the two 22 3/4" door and drawer opening stiles, and the two 5 3/4" centre shelf face stiles. Join every joint with 3/4" pocket holes and 1 1/4" screws, checking the diagonals before you lift it off the bench, because a frame squared on a flat surface is far easier than one framed onto a standing box. Glue the finished frame to the carcass and pin it with 1 1/4" brads, keeping the stiles flush with the outside faces of the plywood sides. Then measure each finished opening and write the numbers down.

Mount the Euro slides with screws — not nails and glue — to the divider and the cabinet side of the drawer bay, so they stay adjustable and removable. Set the two halves of the pair level with each other and square to the face frame, and follow the hardware's own instructions for setback from the front. Read the slide's required side clearance before you build the box, and note that these are 16" slides: the drawer box has to run at least 16" front to back to use them.

Measure the drawer opening you actually have, subtract the clearance your slides require on each side, and build the box to that. Do not build to the cut list here: it gives 5 1/2" drawer sides and a 4 3/4" x 17 3/4" bottom, which makes a box only about 5 3/4" deep front to back — nowhere near the 16" the specified slides need. Join the 1/2" stock with 1/2" pocket holes and 1" pocket hole screws, and pin the 1/4" plywood bottom on with 1 1/4" brads and glue. Check the box's diagonals before the glue sets; a racked box binds in its slides no matter how carefully the slides were mounted. Set the finished box back 3/4" from the face frame so the drawer front has somewhere to land.

Build each shaker door from two 16" stiles and two 9 3/4" rails, joined with pocket holes and glue on the back face, then fit the 1/2" plywood panel. Dry-fit before gluing: the 2 1/2"-wide frame parts leave a 9 3/4" x 11" opening while the listed panel is 10 3/4" x 16", so the panel cannot sit inside the frame as listed and is meant to be applied to its back. Decide which you are building and size the finished door to the opening you measured rather than to the plan's numbers — plywood thickness and face frame reveal both shift the real opening.

Hang the door on the non-mortise hinges and shim it until the reveal around it is even top to bottom. Attach the drawer front from inside the box so no fastener shows, holding an even gap with shims while you drive the first two screws. Then add the knobs and pulls, marking their positions from a story stick rather than from each door's own edge so they line up across the whole vanity. Note that the summary describes one door and one drawer while the cut list and the hinge count describe two doors — build to whatever openings your face frame actually has.

Sand every surface through the grits, easing the plywood edges and the face frame arrises. Fill the brad holes, sand them flush, and vacuum before finishing. Prime and paint, or stain and seal — and finish the inside of the cabinet and the underside of the centre shelf as well. A bathroom vanity lives in steam, and an unsealed plywood edge will swell and delaminate long before the painted face shows any wear.
materials: Paint, primer or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Add the back supports and nailers
Build and attach the face frame
Fit the door and drawer front, add hardware

flags: ZERO-SLACK LUMBER (verified with packBoards, 1/8" kerf): the three 8 ft 1x3s are exactly consumed by their seven parts — one board packs to a full 96" with no allowance for squaring a factory end. Stated in the prose.

CROWN MOULDING IS TIGHT: the 48" front plus two returns the depth of the 11-1/4" top board is about 70-1/2" of a 72" stick, leaving roughly 1-1/2" before any miter waste. Stated in the prose so it can be checked before cutting.

The 44" side trim is the same length as the 44" front decorative pieces, so the plan does not determine whether those two boards return around the ends or sit in the same band across the 48" top; the rewrite says to dry-fit and mark rather than asserting one reading.
No wall-mounting hardware appears in materials[] even though the description says the mantel mounts to a wall on its own.
Verified sound and stated in the prose: the 44" front pieces leave 2" of the 48" top overhanging at each end, and the 51" back depth board projects 1-1/2" past each end of that same 48" top.
Verified as sufficient: the 8 ft 1x12, the 6 ft 1x10 (two 32-3/4" legs), the 10 ft 1x6, the 4 ft 1x4 and the 6 ft 1x8 each cover their parts.

step.title: Cut the boards
step.body: Cut the top board 48" from the 1x12 and the two legs 32 3/4" from the 1x10. From the 1x3 cut four front decorative pieces 44", the lower front trim 33 1/4", and two back blocking pieces 7 3/4"; cut the two side trim pieces 44" from the 1x6, the upper front trim 33 1/4" from the 1x4, and the back depth board 51" from the 1x8. The three 8 ft 1x3s are exactly consumed by their parts, with one board packing out to a full 96" — square one factory end of each and work from it rather than assuming both ends are true, because there is no slack to recover a bad cut. Leave the crown and the optional edge moulding long until the mantel is assembled and you can measure the actual corners.

ops.body: Lay the 48" top board face down and stand a 32 3/4" leg under each end, square to the top and set in far enough that the trim will cover the joint. Fasten from the back side only — pocket holes into the top edge of each leg if you have a jig, otherwise glue plus finish nails driven where trim will hide them — so the visible top face stays free of fasteners. Measure from each end of the top to its leg and make the two readings match, then check the assembly sits flat on the bench before the glue sets.

Fasten the 51" back depth board and the two 7 3/4" back blocking pieces behind the legs and top; these are what bring the mantel out from the wall to its full depth. The back board runs 51" against the 48" top, so it projects 1 1/2" past each end — set that overhang deliberately and equally, since the back board is what registers against the wall when the mantel is hung. Glue and screw or nail from the back, and keep the fasteners clear of where you will later drive the wall screws.

Hold the crown moulding against the top of the front assembly to see where its bottom edge will land, then position the 33 1/4" upper front trim so about 2 1/2" of it stays visible below the crown once it is mitered on. Mark that reveal line right across the face before fastening anything, and set the 33 1/4" lower front trim from the same line. The whole layered face is built off this one line, so a mark placed by eye here moves every piece above and below it.

Glue the four 44" 1x3 decorative pieces to the front face, stacked edge to edge, and clamp or weight them until the glue grabs. Glue only, no nails: 3/4" x 2 1/2" trim splits readily this close to its edges, and a face-to-face glue joint is stronger than any brad you could put through it. At 44" these pieces leave 2" of the 48" top overhanging at each end — measure that at both ends before the glue sets, because once these four boards are down the front cannot be shifted.

Nail the two 44" 1x6 side trim boards on, keeping their outside edges flush with the pieces they meet. They share the 44" length of the decorative front pieces, so dry-fit them against the assembly and mark their position rather than assuming they return around the ends — the plan's numbers do not settle that. Use 1 1/4" brads and glue, setting the nail heads below the surface so filler will hide them, and check that the front and side pieces meet without a step, since the crown returns land on that corner and will telegraph any gap.

Miter the crown's outside corners with the moulding held against the saw fence at its actual spring angle, not laid flat — crown cut flat leaves a gap at the corner that no amount of filler will hide. Cut a test corner in an offcut first and check it closes. Watch the stock as you go: the 48" front plus two returns the depth of the 11 1/4" top comes to about 70 1/2" of a 72" stick, so there is barely an inch and a half of slack before miter waste and one bad cut costs you the piece. Glue every joint and pin with 1 1/4" brads.

If you want the inset detail, lay the edge moulding out as a square on the front face, centred within the decorative band, and miter all four corners. Cut all four pieces before gluing any of them, so you can adjust the square as a set rather than chasing an error around it. Glue it on rather than nailing — the moulding is thin and the surface behind it is already trim over trim, so a brad has nothing solid to bite.

Fill every nail hole, sand through the grits, and pay particular attention to the built-up front, where the seams between the decorative pieces are the detail people actually look at. Vacuum and wipe the dust off, then prime and paint, or stain and seal. Secure the finished mantel to the wall by screwing through the back depth board into studs — a piece this size is stable standing but should never be left free to tip.
materials: Primer and paint, or stain and poly

baseStepTitles: Lay out the top and legs
Glue on the decorative front pieces
